notes.anchor: a little Greek bond in my own hand
notes.note: Cicero's joke: a chirographum (a bond written in one's own hand) drawn up Graeculam, in informal Greek style, was less binding at Roman law than a formal sealed instrument. He teases the jurist Trebatius that his own promise is therefore as 'flimsy' as the guarantees Trebatius complains of.
A reused writing surface, scraped clean of an earlier text. Cicero mock-praises Trebatius's thrift, then needles him: what could have been worth erasing except his own legal formulae?

More Romano: with the full formality of a Roman letter of recommendation (commendatio), as opposed to the casual Greek-style note joked about above. Recommending a friend to a patron was a structured social obligation.

notes.anchor: the buzzing of my own constituents
notes.note: An elaborate joke: Cicero pretends the croaking frogs of the marshes around the near-deserted town of Ulubrae are his loyal voters turning out to honour him. Trebatius had apparently helped 'procure' him this imaginary constituency.

notes.anchor: The sixth day before the Ides of April
notes.note: 8 April by inclusive Roman reckoning (the Ides of April fell on the 13th). Romans counted dates backward from the Kalends, Nones, and Ides, including both endpoints.

notes.anchor: read out in a public assembly
notes.note: A contio was a non-voting public meeting at which magistrates addressed the people. Cicero means Trebatius's letter was so innocuous it could have been published openly, yet Trebatius had asked him to destroy it.
